skill.name: context-tools
skill.description: Context management tools for Claude Code, Claude, and Codex that index large codebases, map symbols, detect similar code, and expose MCP queries for fast navigation.
skill.summary: Index large codebases and query symbols through MCP-backed repository maps.
skill.author: ChipFlow

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and blocker patterns, but most high-severity blocker hits are false positives from documentation, hashes, duplicate-detection wording, or local examples. Confirmed risks are legitimate but sensitive local behavior: automatic hooks, local subprocess indexing, broad source-file reads, local SQLite storage, and project `.claude` writes. No evidence found of malicious prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or unauthorized outbound network calls.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Hook Execution and Project Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill runs shell hooks that create `.claude`, generate manifests, inspect local SQLite state, and remove old plugin cache directories. This is expected for a context-management plugin but should be disclosed because it changes local project and plugin-cache state automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook and write paths are explicit and confirmed in executable shell and Python files. The commands are mostly quoted and local, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Background Indexer Subprocess Reads and Stores Source Metadata
medium_findings.description: The MCP server spawns a local indexer with `uv run`, scans Python, Rust, and C++ source files, and writes repository maps, caches, and SQLite indexes under `.claude`. This creates useful local context but can expose private source structure through generated artifacts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess launch, source discovery, and artifact writes are directly visible. No network destination is present, so the concern is local privacy and process behavior.

medium_findings.title: SQLite-Controlled File Path Used for Source Retrieval
medium_findings.description: The symbol-content tool builds a file path from `project_root` plus `file_path` read from the SQLite index, then reads that file. If a local attacker or compromised project modifies the index, this may read files outside the intended source set through path traversal.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path is taken from a local database row and read without a visible containment check. Exploitability depends on the attacker being able to tamper with the project-local SQLite database.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Static References
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded links in README installation and badge documentation. No runtime network call or data upload was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation links or installation instructions. They do not execute network requests by themselves.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reviewed hash usage computes SHA256 content hashes for cache invalidation and integrity tracking, not password storage or security authentication. No weak cryptographic primitive was confirmed in the inspected context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses SHA256 for file content cache checks, which is appropriate for change detection. The static blocker label overstates the actual risk.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Findings Are Duplicate-Detection Terminology
low_findings.description: The static C2 keyword hits occur in commands that parse and print similar classes or functions from repository maps. The context is local duplicate review, not command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines process local clash summaries and ignored pairs. I found no network listener, beacon, or remote control logic in this context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Launch for Indexing
dangerous_patterns.description: The MCP server starts `uv run generate-repo-map.py` as a subprocess for repository indexing. Arguments are fixed around the plugin script and project root, which lowers injection risk but still executes local code automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct `subprocess.Popen` usage is confirmed. It does not use `shell=True`, so the main risk is automatic execution rather than shell injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Background Reindex with nohup
dangerous_patterns.description: The cache checker can start a background reindexer using `nohup uv run` when it determines that source files changed. This persistent background behavior should be visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The background command is explicit in the shell script. It is scoped to local reindexing and writes logs under `.claude`.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Local Source Index Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: The indexer writes repository maps, progress files, caches, and SQLite symbol data into `.claude`. These files may contain private source names, signatures, docstrings, and paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The generated file list is documented and the write calls are visible. This is a clear local data exposure consideration, not evidence of exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Large repositories make it hard for AI coding tools to find the right symbols and project context. This skill builds local repository maps and MCP symbol queries so Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can navigate code faster.
actual_capabilities: Builds a project manifest with detected languages, build systems, entry points, and recent Git activity.
Indexes Python, Rust, and C++ symbols into local repository map files and a SQLite database.
Provides MCP tools for symbol search, file symbol lists, source retrieval, reindexing, and index status checks.
Detects potentially similar classes and functions using local symbol metadata and fuzzy matching.
Stores project-specific learnings in `.claude/learnings.md` and prompts before context compaction.
Runs background indexing with resource limits and watchdog checks for hung indexer processes.
limitations: Only Python, Rust, and C++ source files are parsed for symbol extraction.
It writes index, cache, manifest, log, and learning files under each project `.claude` directory.
The MCP server and hooks require `uv`, Python 3.10 or newer, and Claude Code plugin support.
Duplicate detection is heuristic and should be reviewed before refactoring decisions.

use_cases.title: Navigate a Large Service Repository
use_cases.description: Find classes, functions, and methods by name pattern without repeatedly scanning the full repository.

use_cases.title: Prepare AI Context Before Refactoring
use_cases.description: Create a compact map of source structure, documentation gaps, and similar implementation areas before a change.

use_cases.title: Retain Project Learnings Across Sessions
use_cases.description: Record important project discoveries so future Claude Code sessions can recover context after compaction.

prompt_templates.title: Check Index Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Use context-tools to check whether the repository map is ready. Summarize the indexed symbol count and any next step.

prompt_templates.title: Find Related Symbols
prompt_templates.prompt: Search this repository for symbols matching [pattern]. Group the results by file and explain which ones look most relevant.

prompt_templates.title: Map a Refactor Target
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the repository map to inspect [class or function]. Find related symbols and summarize the likely impact area.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Similar Implementations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the repository map for similar classes or functions. Identify likely duplicates, false positives, and safe consolidation candidates.

output_examples.input: Find configuration-related classes in this repository.
output: A grouped list of matching classes by file.
A short note about each class signature and docstring.

output_examples.input: Show what changed in the project context after indexing.
output: A manifest summary with detected languages and build systems.
A repository map status with symbol count and freshness.

output_examples.input: Review possible duplicate functions before refactoring.
output: A prioritized list of similar functions with locations.
Reasoning for likely duplicate or acceptable similarity.
Suggested review order for a human maintainer.

best_practices: Run or verify indexing before asking for symbol-level navigation in a large repository.
Treat duplicate findings as review prompts, not automatic refactor instructions.
Review generated `.claude` files before sharing a project archive.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on this skill for languages outside its parser support without confirming coverage.
Do not publish generated repository maps if they contain private source structure.
Do not use MCP symbol results as a substitute for tests, type checks, or code review.

faq.question: Does this skill send code to a remote service?
faq.answer: No evidence of runtime code exfiltration was found. It stores indexes locally under the project `.claude` directory.

faq.question: Which languages does it index?
faq.answer: It indexes Python, Rust, and C++ files. Other files may appear in manifests but are not symbol parsed.

faq.question: What files does it create?
faq.answer: It can create project manifests, repository maps, SQLite indexes, cache files, progress files, logs, and learnings under `.claude`.

faq.question: Does it work with Codex?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata marks Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support, but the plugin is primarily written for Claude Code MCP workflows.

faq.question: Can it detect duplicate code automatically?
faq.answer: It can flag similar classes and functions. A maintainer should verify each finding before changing code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: It has legitimate local indexing behavior, but it also runs hooks, spawns subprocesses, and reads source content from project paths.
